data.title: Russia dismisses Trump tariff threats on Cuba: 'We don't have much trade'
data.description:
The Kremlin on Thursday said it is discussing options with Cuba to provide assistance to the island in the face of a President Trump threatening tariffs on any country providing oil to Havana. 

Kremlin Spokesperson Dmitry Peskov said it is “impossible to discuss these issues in such a public manner,” according to Russian state news outlet RIA Novosti.

Peskov suggested that Russia could be insulated from Trump’s tariff threats since economic ties between the U.S. and Russia were largely severed over Moscow’s 2022 full-scale invasion of Ukraine. 

“We wouldn’t want any escalation, but on the other hand, we don’t have much trade right now,” Peskov said. 

Trump’s attempt to cut off oil exports to has exacerbated an economic crisis and raised dire warnings of a coming humanitarian crisis. Trump levied the threats against Cuba after launching a military operation capturing Venezuela’s President Nicolas Maduro in January and asserting control of the country’s oil exports. Trump suggested that Havana’s leaders, dependent on subsidized Venezuelan oil, would fall next. 

Russia began evacuating its citizens from Cuba but has been forced to adjust its flight schedules because of the fuel shortage. 

But Russia’s Ambassador to Cuba Viktor Koronelli said that embassy evacuations are not on the agenda and that Moscow “will not abandon Cuba.” 

“In light of the extremely difficult energy situation in Cuba, they are counting on help, on a friendly hand from brotherly peoples. And among them, of course, Russia stands in first place,” he said.

The fuel crisis has pushed up food prices for Cubans, led to severe fuel shortages and major power cuts throughout the country, the United Nations said last week. 

The State Department last week said it was delivering $6 million in humanitarian support through two Christian charities “in direct assistance for the Cuban people.”

“We remain vigilant in tracking any diversion or frustration of U.S. assistance efforts, and the regime will be accountable to the United States and its own people for any interference,” it said at the time.

The U.S. embargo has triggered some backlash over humanitarian concerns, with Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum criticising the U.S. for acting “in a very unfair manner.”

“No one can ignore the situation that the Cuban people are currently experiencing because of the sanctions that the United States is imposing in a very unfair manner,” she said during a news conference on Monday, according to the New York Times. 

On Thursday, two Mexican-flagged ships entered Havana harbor loaded with humanitarian aid, Reuters reported. 

Also on Thursday, Chilean Foreign Affairs Minister Alberto van Klaveren said the country will provide monetary assistance for Cuba through multilateral organizations. 

“We all know that there is a very complex situation in Cuba, that there is a population suffering from hunger and that there are also constant power outages,” van Klaveren said, according to Bloomberg.

data.title: Another grand jury fails to indict Trump's political enemies — move on!
data.description:
A federal grand jury this week refused to indict six congressional Democrats over a video where they told service members to reject “illegal orders.”  

Remember that? This is a big deal, not just politically, but legally.  

Sources familiar with the case tell Axios that the Justice Department sought to charge the lawmakers under a statute that carries up to 10 years in prison for anyone who “advises, counsels, urges” insubordination in the military. In the 90-second video posted in November, the six congressional leaders reminded members of the military and intelligence community that “no one has to carry out orders that violate the law or our Constitution.” They did not reference a specific directive.  

After the video was posted, the president called their comments “seditious” and demanded they be “arrested and put on trial.” But now a grand jury declined to indict.  

That outcome fits a broader pattern. Trump’s retribution campaign is hitting a losing streak.  

Federal grand juries almost always return indictments. In 2013, they rejected them just five times out of more than 165,000 cases nationwide, according to the Bureau of Justice Statistics. Prosecutors need only 12 of 23 jurors to agree. The bar is not especially high.  

And yet, in several high-profile cases involving the president’s perceived political adversaries, that bar has not been cleared — or the cases have fallen apart in court.  

For example, charges against former FBI Director James Comey were partially rejected by a grand jury and ultimately dropped after a federal judge ruled that the appointment of hand-picked prosecutor Lindsey Halligan was invalid. In a separate case against New York Attorney General Letitia James, a judge wrote that “all actions flowing from” Halligan’s “defective appointment” are “unlawful exercises of executive power and must be set aside.” A grand jury later declined to indict James a second time.  

Even outside the political arena, juries have pushed back. In November, a former DOJ employee accused of throwing “a sub-style sandwich” at a federal agent was found not guilty. That’s after Pam Bondi pushed to charge him with a felony.  

It’s important to be clear: a grand jury’s refusal to indict is not a declaration of innocence. It simply means prosecutors did not present enough evidence to establish probable cause. But when grand juries — which historically approve the overwhelming majority of federal charges — repeatedly decline to move forward in politically charged cases, that should signal something for the administration.   

The justice system is designed to be slow, procedural and insulated from political pressure. Threats and social media posts don’t substitute for statutory elements or evidentiary standards. Intent must be proven. Appointments must be lawful. Charges must survive judicial scrutiny.  

So while rhetoric may escalate, the courts and juries so far are drawing lines. Maybe it’s time for the administration to take note and set their sights on something other than locking up its perceived political enemies. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Thomas Massie slams Pam Bondi, says Epstein files still being hidden
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie is demanding answers from Attorney General Pam Bondi and others whom he says are still hiding the truth of the Epstein files from the public. In an interview with Zach Weissmueller, my colleague at Reason magazine, Massie explained why he is willing to have this fight, even if it costs him his job in Congress. Take a look. 

Massie is one of the most libertarian-leaning members of Congress, and also the foremost advocate, along with Rep. Ro Khanna, of releasing the Epstein files. He has regularly made appearances alongside purported survivors of Epstein and demanded the Department of Justice provide information on sexual abusers who participated in Epstein’s crimes. Massie and Khanna accused the feds of failing to properly disclose documents relating to Epstein, a violation of the law, either due to incompetence or maliciousness. 

It seems clear that, unlike so many other political actors who have taken up the cause of Epstein disclosure for self-serving partisan reasons — namely, a quest to disparage the reputations of the Clintons and/or Donald Trump, depending on one’s own partisan valence — Massie and Khanna are engaged in a sincere effort to arrive at the truth. Their complaints that DOJ has mishandled Epstein transparency are well-taken. Bondi previously claimed that she had the Epstein client list “sitting on her desk”; in Epstein parlance, this was taken to mean that there was a list of elite political figures, academics and businessmen for whom Epstein procured underage girls. But no such document has ever been released, and FBI Director Kash Patel subsequently stated that there is no one else involved in Epstein’s sex crimes. 

Most people demanding more answers about Epstein have alleged a vast and sinister conspiracy, involving not just Epstein but other powerful abusers. At yesterday’s House Judiciary hearing, lawmakers accused Bondi of failing not just Epstein’s victims, but hundreds of young women victimized by Epstein and his associates. 

If there are other people who sexually abused underage girls, the public ought to know about it. But that remains an open question, since none of the files substantiate the idea that Epstein procured underage girls for a cabal of sexual abusers. 

The files do, however, reveal the names of people who met with Epstein, took money from Epstein, or continued to email with Epstein over the years. These people are not accused of crimes; in many cases, the idea that the named individual did something particularly bad seems to spring from a misconception.  

In other words, releasing the files has resulted in some real world harms. 

One of those folks is Mark Tramo. Tramo is an associate adjunct professor of neurology at the University of California, Los Angeles. Whether he will remain in that position is unknown. A sign-wielding woman has reportedly camped herself outside the building where he works, and has demanded that UCLA fire him. 

What was Tramo’s crime? Nothing. No one has accused him of any crime whatsoever. His moral failing, according to his critics, is that he emailed Epstein with some frequency over the years, including about his scientific research and areas of academic interest. 

In one such email, he mentioned reading a study that found “a newborn will suck on a pacifier more vigorously” if the baby hears his own mother’s voice rather than some other woman’s. 

Judging by the reaction on X, social media users have found this email highly incriminating. It’s what the woman with the sign referenced when she screamed at the department that Tramo should lose his job. 

The conservative writer Rod Dreher is with her, writing: “God bless this woman! What kind of monster must Mark Tramo be?” 

People seem to have imagined that Tramo was giving Epstein advice about something very graphic. But this notion is patently absurd. 

Tramo was a professor of neurology. 

He had co-authored articles on the effects of music on premature babies. He had received funding from Epstein to create a research institution for the study of music and the brain. The idea that he was assisting Epstein in the sexual abuse of babies — instead of just sharing an interesting fact related to his area of academic expertise — is extremely far-fetched.  

Frankly, the idea that Epstein would have been interested in this knowledge for disgusting sexual reasons is also quite doubtful. Epstein was convicted of sexual abusing teenage girls; there is no evidence he was sexually attracted to babies. 

Nevertheless, since Tramo’s communications with Epstein continued after the disgraced financier’s 2008 incarceration for sexual misconduct, he is among the names of people considered to have associated with a known abuser. In statements to the media, Tramo denied having knowledge of Epstein’s specific crimes, saying that he thought Epstein had been convicted of prostitution. 

He said: “I myself had not heard anything [about] statutory rape or minors being involved, I never saw him with young girls, never visited the island, never flew in his plane. I was introduced to him by the Harvard Provost—he donated several millions to Harvard and was dangling a lot more.” 

It is unclear if UCLA is taking any action against Tramo, though the university website did remove his profile page on its media list of designated field experts. UCLA did not immediately respond to a request for comment. 

In the meantime, Tramo told me via email that he has been besieged with hate mail, including death threats. 

Now Tramo, for what it’s worth, asserts that he did not have specific knowledge about the nature of Epstein’s crimes, the details of which were mostly unknown to the public until a 2018 expose in The Miami-Herald. 

Massie takes the view, however that anyone who associated with Epstein after 2018 is fair game.

Is that fair? You be the judge. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Live updates: Dems block DHS funding bill; Judge rejects Pentagon attempt to punish Kelly
data.description:
Senate Democrats blocked GOP efforts to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Thursday, putting the department on the brink of a shutdown.

Democrats blocked the legislation after rejecting a White House offer they said did not go far enough to reform the DHS’s immigration enforcement tactics after the fatal shootings of two Minneapolis residents in separate incidents by federal agents last month.

Minnesota government officials and the leaders of the administration’s top immigration enforcement agencies were also grilled by senators earlier Thursday on the administration’s probe of fraud allegations in the state’s social services programs and immigration operations. Though, Border czar Tom Homan said earlier in the day that “Operation Metro Surge” was coming to a close.

A federal judge on Thursday also denied the Pentagon’s efforts to censure Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.) and lower his retirement rank after a controversial video showed the senator and five others calling on service members to reject “illegal orders.” The move comes days after a grand jury failed to indict the lawmakers.

At the White House, President Trump and Environmental Protection Agency Administrator Lee Zeldin announced the end to the 2009 climate change endangerment finding.

Follow along live below.

data.title: Los Angeles neighborhood installing sirens to warn of ICE activity
data.description:
(KTLA) — Residents and community activists in Los Angeles’s Highland Park are working to install warning sirens to alert the neighborhood when federal immigration agents are spotted nearby. 

Organizers say the goal is simple: Give families time to move to safety.

Amanda Alcalde created the Highland Park Community Support Group, offering resources to residents concerned about federal immigration raids.

“We’d like to ultimately have this along all the different streets so they can take shelter,” she said.

Alcalde has already posted flyers alerting neighbors about the sirens, which she hopes to begin installing later this month. Some of the notices have been posted on social media.

One of the most recent posts indicates that a siren will be installed on Feb. 23 at the corner of Monte Vista Street and Avenue 56. A dual-language school sits at that intersection.

“If you hear the siren, ICE is in the community,” the flyer reads.

Because the effort isn’t city-sanctioned, organizers say they’re working directly with willing homeowners and local businesses, placing the sirens on private property rather than going through city permitting channels. The nonprofit organization’s efforts are also being funded by donations from community members.

“Really taking a lot of that influence from Minneapolis and trying to turn it into our own here,” Alcalde said.

A different type of “alarm” will also be present on the streets. Volunteers distributed ICE alert whistles to neighbors, encouraging residents to warn each other when enforcement activity is seen.

“We don’t directly get ourselves involved with ICE, but we will get involved protecting the community to stay in their office or home,” activist David Trujillo said.

Federal officials have not commented specifically on the siren initiative.

Locally, this move also follows a new directive from Mayor Karen Bass aimed at limiting ICE activity on city property, part of the city’s broader response to increased immigration enforcement across Los Angeles.

“I’ve seen a lot of fear in people’s eyes. I don’t see a lot of our ethnic minorities out in the day-to-day. It’s big change. It feels dystopian in a way,” Alcalde said.

data.title: NJ governor signing order banning ICE from launching actions on state properties
data.description:
New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill (D) on Wednesday signed an executive order banning Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) from undertaking operations on state property. 

Sherrill’s order makes an exception for immigration operations authorized by a judicial warrant, reflecting a broader push from Democrats in the House to have the agency require a judge’s approval for ICE actions nationwide.

The newly elected governor said she was signing the order amid “ICE’s willingness to flout the Constitution and violently endanger communities – detaining children, arresting citizens, and even killing several innocent civilians,” according to a press release. 

Sherrill’s measure follows the killings of two U.S. citizens in Minnesota be federal immigration authorities in separate incidents. Their deaths sparked protests across the country and backlash from lawmakers on the Hill.

On Wednesday, the Garden State governor said “no ICE staging areas, no processing locations, no operating bases, no federal civil immigration actions” will run from “any state grounds — not our parks, not our roadways, not our buildings.”

In addition to the ban on ICE actions across state property, the New Jersey Office of the Attorney General will launch a portal for New Jersey residents to upload reports about potential misconduct from ICE officials or violations of the law. 

The portal gives residents the chance to report their interactions with immigration authorities in a total of 10 languages. 

“It is essential that federal agents who conduct immigration enforcement operations in New Jersey follow the Constitution, as well as federal and state laws. When federal immigration officers violate the law and sow fear in our communities, they undermine public trust in law enforcement and make it harder for us to keep the public safe,” Acting Attorney General Jennifer Davenport said in a statement. 

“We encourage the public to share photos and videos through our new online portal.  We will ensure the safety of our communities and hold the federal government accountable when they violate the law,” she added.

data.title: Shutdown imminent after Senate Democrats block Homeland Security bill
data.description:
Senate Democrats voted Thursday to block a motion to advance a House-passed bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security, putting Washington on the brink of a partial government shutdown that will affect more than 260,000 federal employees.

The motion, which required 60 votes, failed to advance by a vote of 52-47.

Democrats blocked the legislation after rejecting an offer from the White House they said didn’t go far enough to reform immigration enforcement operations after the fatal shootings of two protesters in Minneapolis last month.

As a result, funding for key agencies such as the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) and the Coast Guard will lapse on Saturday without further action from Congress.

The agencies that are the main targets of Democratic fury, however, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Customs and Border Protection (CBP), will be able to continue operations without much disruption.

Both agencies received tens of billions of dollars through the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which President Trump signed into law last year.

“Democrats have been very clear. We will not support an extension of the status quo, a status quo that permits masked secret police to barge into people’s homes without warrants, no guardrails, zero oversight from independent authorities,” Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said before the vote.

Schumer acknowledged that White House border czar Tom Homan announced on Thursday that it was ending its surge deployment of ICE officers in Minnesota but declared the action falls short of what’s needed to prevent troubling incidents connected to law enforcement operations.

“We need legislation to rein in ICE and end the violence,” he said. “Without legislation, what Tom Homan says today could be reversed tomorrow on a whim from a Donald Trump.”

Senate appropriators say that TSA agents and federal workers at FEMA and the Coast Guard will be able to keep working in a “limited” capacity and are looking into what extent that federal operations will be limited. 

Republican lawmakers predict that delays at airports may be the most immediate impact felt by the public. 

A GOP senator said that Sen. Katie Britt (R-Ala.) will ask for unanimous consent to pass a two-week extension of Homeland Security funding through the end of the month but Democrats are expected to object to it.

Senate Republican Whip John Barrasso (R-Wyo.) accused Democrats of “flip-flopping” on the full-year Homeland Security funding measure after signing off on it last month.

“What we see are Democrats flip-flopping on funding the government. This was a bipartisan bill. Democrats helped negotiate it,” he said on the floor, venting exasperation felt by Republican members of the Appropriations Committee.

Sen. Patty Murray (D-Wash.), the ranking member of the Appropriations panel, last month defended the Homeland Security appropriations bill, arguing to colleagues that blocking it wouldn’t impact ICE and CBP operations in Minnesota.

But the political dynamic for Democrats changed on Jan. 24 when video footage emerged of two federal officers fatally shooting 37-year-old nurse Alex Pretti in the back after another officer confiscated a concealed pistol that he was licensed to carry. Pretti was holding his phone and his glasses while kneeling on the ground when the first shots were fired.

“It is clear to just about everyone in every part of the country that ICE and CBP are out of control and must be reined in,” Murray said on the Senate floor before the vote.

Democrats last week unveiled a 10-point plan for reforming immigration enforcement operations.

They called for a stop to roving ICE patrols, a requirement that federal immigration officers obtain search warrants before entering a suspect’s home, and a prohibition on federal agents wearing masks to hide their identities.

They also demanded establishing universal use-of-force standards for federal officers, regulation and standardization of uniforms and requirements for officers to wear body cameras and proper identification.

The White House responded earlier this week with a one-page letter offering some concessions and then followed up by sending legislative text to Democratic negotiators in hopes of reaching a compromise.

But Democrats swiftly rejected the proposal.

“They did not address our major concerns. We’re going through it right now and intend to offer a counteroffer,” Murray told The Hill Thursday.

Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-N.H.), a key moderate who voted in November to end the record-breaking 43-day government shutdown, said Thursday morning that she will not vote for another temporary funding extension for the Homeland Security Department.

“I’m not going to vote for a CR until we see some progress on reforms. It’s not acceptable that we have a federal agency killing American citizens in the streets and we’re not taking any action,” she said.

Asked what the White House is willing to do to rein in ICE officers, Shaheen responded: “Nothing that I’ve heard.”

data.title: Trump repeals landmark finding that climate change endangers the public
data.description:
The Trump administration on Thursday repealed the landmark 2009 legal finding that climate change poses a threat to the public — and axed climate rules for cars and trucks that were regulated under the finding.

The repeal is an aggressive escalation in the administration’s efforts both to deny climate change’s impacts and to remove environmental regulations that it says will hold back the U.S. economy.

While the first Trump administration weakened vehicle emissions standards, Thursday’s action gets rid of them entirely — meaning automakers will be under no federal obligation to reduce their climate impacts. 

The first Trump administration also did not go after the now-repealed finding that greenhouse gases pose a threat to public health, known as the endangerment finding.

The endangerment finding has legal significance, unpinning the nation’s climate change regulations.

The Clean Air Act requires the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) administrator to regulate motor vehicle emissions of any pollutant that “in his judgment cause, or contribute to, air pollution which may reasonably be anticipated to endanger public health or welfare.”

The Supreme Court ruled in 2007 that planet-warming emissions fall under the law’s definition of air pollutants and should be regulated if they’re found to be a threat to public health.

In 2009, the Obama administration determined that they do, in fact, pose a threat, setting up the EPA’s authority to curb climate pollution.

At the time, the Obama administration based its finding on a determination that “increases in ambient ozone are expected to occur over broad areas of the country, and they are expected to increase serious adverse health effects in large population areas.” 

It also cited “the impact on mortality and morbidity associated with increases in average temperatures, which increase the likelihood of heat waves” and “the evidence concerning how human-induced climate change may alter extreme weather events.”

The finding’s repeal represents a major piece of the Trump administration’s push to overturn environmental regulations and comes as the president and his allies have repeatedly denied or downplayed the impacts of climate change. 

There is a consensus in the scientific community that human activity, especially the burning of fossil fuels, is heating up the planet and worsening extreme weather. 

Some climate damage could be irreversible if the planet heats up enough to cross “tipping points,” such as significant melting of ice sheets that can’t be reformed.

President Trump campaigned on overturning Biden-era regulations that were so stringent that they were expected to significantly push the market toward electric vehicles and hybrids and away from gas-powered cars.

“This will be the largest deregulatory action in American history,” White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Tuesday when announcing the change, adding it will save drivers money on the cost of new vehicles.

“This is just one more way this administration is working to make life more affordable for everyday Americans,” Leavitt said.

It estimated that the standards, now wiped out under Trump, could have prevented up to 7.2 billion tons of carbon dioxide emissions through 2055 — about four times the emissions of the whole U.S. transportation sector in 2021. 

Transportation is the largest source of planet-warming emissions in the U.S., making up 28 percent of the nation’s contribution to climate change in 2022.

In addition to mitigating climate change, the Biden-era car rules were also expected to reduce other types of pollution — preventing up to  2,500 premature deaths in the year 2055 and also reducing the number of heart attacks.

These regulations are not the only major climate rules the administration is considering zeroing out. Last year, it also proposed to eliminate all climate regulations on power plants, the nation’s second largest emissions source.

data.title: Texas smokehouses squeezed as beef supply tightens nationwide
data.description:
DALLAS (NewsNation) – Texas pitmasters warn the state’s barbecue industry is under growing pressure as droughts, thinning herds and supply delays drive up beef prices and force some smokehouses out of business.

Over the past six to nine months, pitmasters say brisket prices have surged dramatically, signaling more sticker shock ahead for customers.

Rising costs that ranchers have faced for years are now making their way onto restaurant menus, increasing prices for brisket, burgers, and other beef staples.

Dwindling cattle supply putting BBQ at risk

At the heart of the issue is a dwindling cattle supply. A recent U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) report revealed the smallest U.S. cattle inventory in 75 years.

The report found that there are only 86.2 million head of cattle and calves on U.S. farms as of Jan. 1, 2026, the lowest number in America since 1951. That number fell 0.4 percent from the previous year, when the herd also reached its lowest level since 1951.

Herds have been shrinking for several years, made worse by drought and disease. As supply tightens, restaurant owners and beef producers say it’s becoming increasingly difficult to maintain profit margins.

For barbecue joints, there’s no easy substitute. Brisket is the centerpiece of Texas barbecue, and rebuilding the nation’s cattle supply takes time

“That’s the lowest number of beef cows we’ve had in the U.S. since going back in the early 1950s,” said Garth Ruff, a beef cattle field specialist at Ohio State University. “You’re looking at 27 to 30 months before I see a product on the shelf. We just can’t speed up the biology of these animals.”

Ranchers fear for survival as costs soar

Small, family-owned ranchers say they’re being squeezed hardest. Many worry they won’t survive the next few years, lacking the scale and pricing power of large corporate operations to absorb rising feed, fuel and labor costs.

While small farms account for about 85% of U.S. farms, large corporations still dominate production.

Americans consume roughly 50 to 60 pounds of beef per year, according to the USDA. But in Texas — where barbecue is a cultural institution — some pitmasters warn that if prices continue climbing, long-standing traditions could face an uncertain future.

data.title: Judge rejects BBC bid to delay discovery in Trump lawsuit
data.description:
A federal judge in Florida who is presiding over President Trump’s lawsuit against the BBC denied a motion Wednesday to delay discovery in the case and set a trial date for next spring.

U.S. District Judge Roy Altman, who was appointed by Trump during his first term in office, in his ruling signaled the outlet’s application was premature. The trial is set to begin in February 2027, according to the documents.

The BBC has previously said it plans to file a motion to dismiss the case before next month.

Trump sued the news organization in December over a documentary the network aired last year that was edited to omit context around comments he made to a crowd of supporters in Washington, D.C. just before the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the U.S. Capitol.

The film showed Trump only saying, “We’re going down to the Capitol, and I’ll be there with you. And we fight. We fight like hell.”

The president’s full remarks that day were: “We’re going to walk down, and I’ll be there with you, we’re going to walk down, we’re going to walk down. Anyone you want, but I think right here, we’re going to walk down to the Capitol, and we’re going to cheer on our brave senators and congressmen and women, and we’re going to be cheering so much for some of them. Because you’ll never take back our country with weakness.” 

The network ousted key members of its leadership after the documentary was published and has publicly apologized to Trump over the piece.

“While the BBC sincerely regrets the manner in which the video clip was edited, we strongly disagree there is a basis for a defamation claim,” a spokesperson for the outlet said after the president sued.

Trump’s attorneys argue that the way the film was edited was “false, deceptive, and defamatory,” and is seeking $5 billion in damages.
